author: mustafasuleyman
text:
What they found:
- MAI-DxO boosted performance of every model tested on those 304 cases
- 85.5% solve rate vs. 20% by a group of physicians
- Its higher accuracy came with LOWER overall testing costs than lone LLMs or physicians

author: mustafasuleyman
text: Microsoft AI built MAI-DxO to simulate a virtual panel of physicians with different approaches collaborating to find a diagnosis on each case. They also included the ability to set a budget to avoid infinite testing (higher costs, longer wait times, etc.).

author: mustafasuleyman
text: While AI has achieved near-perfect scores on the US Medical Licensing Exam, we set a higher benchmark: 304 cases from the New England Journal of Medicine. These are some of the toughest and most diagnostically complex cases a physician can face.

author: mustafasuleyman
text: We're taking a big step towards medical superintelligence. AI models have aced multiple choice medical exams – but real patients don’t come with ABC answer options. Now MAI-DxO can solve some of the world’s toughest open-ended cases with higher accuracy and lower costs.

author: mustafasuleyman
mustafasuleyman
text: It’s the kind of 1:1 personal tutoring that’s been historically out of reach for most of the world’s students (and impossible for often-overloaded teachers to offer). But now, thanks to AI, it doesn’t have to be.

author: mustafasuleyman
text: What it does mean: AI personalizing education is going to be a game-changer for students. Imagine personalized textbooks for those fast readers out there. Or being able to ask a podcast for clarification or for things to be explained in a different way, again and again

author: mustafasuleyman
text: So does this mean the future of edu (besides AP Gov…) is all personalized podcasts, all the time? No. Some people will want visuals or aren’t auditory learners. Some people are faster readers than they are listeners and will always go for the time-saving option.

author: mustafasuleyman
text:
My takeaways:
- Personalization can have a huge impact
- We shouldn’t underweight the importance of helping students ENJOY learning
- Not all subjects are created equal for personalization!

author: mustafasuleyman
text: For philosophy and psych, the group that got personalized podcasts saw it pay off – they scored higher on comprehension quizzes than students who learned from either the non-personalized podcasts or the textbook.

author: mustafasuleyman
text: After that, students in each group had to take a comprehension quiz on the chapter topic, plus a survey about their experience. What they found gets me excited about the future of learning.

author: mustafasuleyman
text:
Researchers took 180 US college students + divided them up:
- 60 read a textbook chapter (on philosophy, psych, or gov)
- 60 listened to an AI-gen podcast about the chapter
- 60 listened to a personalized AI-gen podcast based on (self-reported) learning style/interests/etc.

author: mustafasuleyman

mustafasuleyman
text: - Yes, higher confidence thresholds meant more questions went unanswered - something we either need to accept, depending on the risk level, or be willing to throw more compute in when confidence is critical

author: mustafasuleyman
text: - Higher compute and confidence were extra important for model performance when wrong answers had serious consequences (like when they were weighted 20x). You wouldn't want a 51%-sure diagnosis of a terminal illness.

author: mustafasuleyman
text: - And good news! More compute = more confidence as well. (However, more compute made one model become more confident in wrong answers too. Something to keep an eye to make sure model confidence doesn’t tip into overconfidence.)

author: mustafasuleyman
text: - Yes, across the board more compute = more accuracy (although without any confidence thresholds, you'll still get wild guesses or wrong answers)

author: mustafasuleyman
text:
They also experimented with different risk levels. On one end of the spectrum: no penalty for wrong answers. On the other: the penalty of wrong answers weighted 20x more than the reward for correct ones.

What they found:

author: mustafasuleyman
text: In the study, they measured how different combinations of compute budget and confidence thresholds (being at least 50% sure of the answer, etc.) affected models’ performance on a benchmark math test.

author: mustafasuleyman
text: We know more compute results in higher accuracy, but are the models more confident those answers ARE accurate too? And how do we teach them when to say “I don’t know”? That’s what the research team wanted to find out.

author: mustafasuleyman
text: You can't just be right, you have to know you're right. Good advice for LLMs, according to new Johns Hopkins research. Sometimes no answer is better than a wrong one – life or death choices in medicine, for example, or big financial decisions.

text: Yes, this is just a single pilot program, but the team's findings get me so excited about what's possible. This is exactly why I'm so passionate about AI—opening up access and opportunity for people.

author: mustafasuleyman
text: • The team also found that the more sessions students attended, the more they benefitted. What could results look like if they had access more than twice a week? For longer than six weeks?

author: mustafasuleyman
text: • The impact of those six weeks didn't fade; in fact, it had a ripple effect. Students from the pilot performed better on final exams months later, across subjects that weren't even covered in the pilot program.

author: mustafasuleyman
text: • Access to AI was a rising tide that lifted all boats, and narrowed the gender gap between male students and the female students who had been trailing behind before the pilot program began.

author: mustafasuleyman
text: • When the researchers compared their results to other educational interventions, this pilot outperformed 80% of them. 80%!

author: mustafasuleyman
text: • Many of these students had never even used a computer before. They spent the beginning of the program figuring out how to navigate a PC, setting up user accounts, being taught how to prompt. Makes the learning curve even more remarkable.

author: mustafasuleyman
text:
The setup: For 6 weeks, students used Copilot in their computer lab 2x/week, guided by teachers on selected topics and grammar/writing tasks. 
The results: A pen and paper test showed their scores improving .3 standard deviations, the equivalent of almost 2 years of learning.

author: mustafasuleyman
text: After Ethan's post, I went on a deep dive into this study! I could go on and on about the results but if I had to boil it down to my biggest takeaways...

author: mustafasuleyman
text: This is exactly why I'm so passionate about AI—opening up access and opportunity for people. Even more astounding to read that many of these students had never even used a computer before this pilot. Gets me so excited for what's possible, for everyone.

text: It's by no means a perfect technology. There's lots more work needed to scale production, not to mention in ensuring they deliver energy that's high-powered, not just long-lasting. But it makes me optimistic. (TBD whether we'll be able to bring these in checked luggage.)

author: mustafasuleyman
text: And Carbon-14 is a renewable resource, a waste product from nuclear energy. Lithium meanwhile is finite, difficult to access, and resource intensive to mine.

author: mustafasuleyman
text: There's huge sustainability upside here—when the batteries do run out of power thousands of years later, they're reusable. Most lithium batteries end up in a landfill.

author: mustafasuleyman
text: It's "bio-compatible" (safe for your body, thanks to the diamond seal) and could eliminate the need to surgically replace batteries in pacemakers and medical devices every 5-10 years. It could power space missions or deep-sea sensors, where replacing a battery is near impossible.

author: mustafasuleyman
text:
It's not the diamond itself that powers the battery—it's small amount of Carbon-14 trapped inside the diamond and slowly decaying.
 
Emphasis on "slowly". Carbon-14's half-life is 5730 years. Your typical lithium-ion battery lasts perhaps a decade (if you're lucky/careful).

author: mustafasuleyman
text: I've been fascinated with batteries' role in sustainability lately (prepare for more battery posts!) and came across this during my reading: scientists at the University of Bristol invented a new diamond-coated battery that lasts thousands of years.
